prompt:
This PR turns the-valley from a design-docs-only repo into a software project: it gains (1) a CUE schema defining what a valley project is — the canonical, Nix-independent declarative format — and (2) a flake exporting a NixOS module that installs a valley host by validating and consuming that CUE config. Owner decisions (patflynn, 2026-07-06): the-valley exports its own host module, consumer machines (the cosmo repo's hosts) only install and minimally configure it, superseding cosmo PR #601 where the module lived in cosmo; and the-valley's native config format is CUE, independent of Nix — Nix is one installer, never the owner of the domain model.

Read first: README.md, design/roadmap.md (Phase 0 and the cross-cutting threads section — note CUE is already the committed schema language for event schemas, so config and events share one schema language), design/user-scenarios.md (S1), .the-valley/README.md (knowledge-node convention), and the implementation you will port: run "gh pr diff 601 --repo patflynn/cosmo" to get the reviewed, hardened module.

What to build:

1. The CUE schema (e.g. schema/ package at the repo root). It defines the valley domain model, starting minimal:
   - #Host (or similarly named top level): projects: [Name=string]: #Project, with project names constrained by the same name-safe pattern PR #601 used for repo names.
   - #Project: git: enable: bool | *true (git is one store type under a project — the only one today; the naming must not hard-code git at the top level, because the design deliberately does not commit the-valley to being git-only), and mirrors: [...string] | *[] (push-mirror URLs).
   - Deployment concerns (data directory, unix user, SSH keys) are NOT in the CUE schema — those are machine integration and belong to the installer. The CUE file describes the valley, not the machine.
   - Include a commented example config (e.g. examples/host.cue) declaring the-valley itself with its GitHub mirror: git@github.com:gunk-dev/the-valley.git.

2. flake.nix exporting nixosModules.default (alias nixosModules.valley-host). Port the module from cosmo PR #601, preserving three pieces of review hardening exactly: (a) the sshd Match block for the git user is terminated with "Match All" so later-appended sshd config is not scoped to the git user; (b) repo initialization is create-only, array-based, and uses a HEAD-file check so a pre-existing empty directory still gets initialized and nothing is ever deleted or overwritten; (c) the post-receive dispatcher exits early on empty stdin and only ever replaces store-symlink hooks, never hand-written ones.

3. The module's option surface is machine-integration only, plus the CUE config as the single domain input:

   services.valley = {
     enable = true;
     user = "git";              # default
     group = "git";             # default
     dataDir = "/srv/git";      # default; consumers point it at a ZFS dataset
     authorizedKeys = [ ... ];  # host-level access; asserted non-empty when enabled
     config = ./valley.cue;     # path to the CUE host config — the canonical declaration
   };

   - At build time the module must vet the CUE file against the shipped schema and export it to JSON (pkgs.cue; a runCommand derivation whose output Nix reads is the standard mechanism), then generate provisioning — the bare-repo init list and per-project mirror hooks — from the exported data. An invalid CUE file must fail the system build with cue's error, not produce a half-configured host. Nix must never redefine the domain schema; if you need the data in Nix, it comes from the cue export.
   - Do NOT add a projects option in Nix. The CUE file is the only way to declare projects.
   - mirrors: for each URL, install a hook into that project's post-receive.d that pushes updated refs to the mirror (git push --mirror is acceptable at v1; note in docs that it propagates deletions, which is the correct semantic for a mirror). Mirror pushes must be best-effort — background them or tolerate failure with a log line — because a dead mirror must never make the primary reject work. Credentials are the host's concern: the module assumes the git user's SSH identity and known_hosts are provisioned by the consumer (cosmo, via its secrets) — document this, do not invent secret plumbing here.
   - Access is deliberately host-level in v1: with a single git user and git-shell, per-project access is not enforceable without forced-command machinery, and a per-project access option would misrepresent the security boundary. Do NOT add per-project access anywhere (CUE or Nix).

4. Flake checks, all must pass with "nix flake check": cue vet of the example config against the schema; an eval-only NixOS check that instantiates the module with the example config (no VM test needed) so option-surface and cue-export regressions are caught.

5. Doc updates in the same PR:
   - README.md: update the Status section — no longer "Design stage. Nothing built yet."; the repo now ships its first artifacts (the CUE schema and the host module), Phase 0 in progress; a sentence or two, keep the README's voice.
   - .the-valley/outcomes/oc-9949561-push-replication.md: the line "The implementation lives in cosmo; it is tracked here." is now wrong — reword to say the module lives in the-valley and consumers (cosmo) install and configure it.
   - A new decision node in .the-valley/decisions/ following the convention in .the-valley/README.md exactly (check how the existing dcr-* nodes derive their ids from slugs and match it; frontmatter: type decision, status decided, created 2026-07-06). It records four decisions and their rationale: the-valley exports the host module, consumers install/configure (supersedes the cosmo-owned approach of cosmo PR #601); the canonical config format is CUE, independent of Nix — the domain model must be legible to agents and tools in any runtime, Nix is an installer, and CUE is already the schema language for events per the roadmap; the declaration unit is a project with git as one nested store type, so the-valley is not committed to being git-only; public exposure and the migration's dual-push are one mechanism (declared per-project push mirrors), while per-project access is deliberately deferred until S5 (first collaborator) because it is not honestly enforceable with a single git user.

Constraints:
- Nothing else in design/ changes. Do not touch design/roadmap.md or other outcome nodes.
- Keep the flake minimal: schema, module, checks — nothing speculative (no valley CLI yet, no packages, no devShell unless the tooling genuinely requires one).
- Verify relative links resolve in the files you touch.

Open a PR; do not merge. The owner reviews shape first: lead the PR description with the CUE schema and an example config, then the Nix option surface (options, types, defaults), then the layering rationale (CUE owns the domain, Nix owns the machine; projects-not-repos; mirrors-as-connective-tissue; host-level access), then note the two doc corrections and the decision node.
